meta.originalTitle: Keeping the water away 洪水防控

children.text: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 14–26, which are based on Reading Passage 2 below.

Recently, winter floods on the rivers of central Europe have been among the worst for 600 to 700 years, and dams and dykes (protective sea walls) have failed to solve the problem. Traditionally, river engineers have tried to get rid of the water quickly, draining it off the land and down to the sea in rivers reengineered as high-performance drains. But however high they build the artificial riverbanks, the floods keep coming back. And when they come, they seem to be worse than ever.

Engineers are now turning to a different plan: to sap the water's destructive strength by dispersing it into fields, forgotten lakes and flood plains. They are reviving river bends and marshes to curb the flow, and even plugging city drains to encourage floodwater to use other means to go underground. Back in the days when rivers took a winding path to the sea, floodwaters lost force and volume while meandering across flood plains and inland deltas, but today the water tends to have a direct passage to the sea. This means that, when it rains in the uplands, the water comes down all at once.

Worse, when the flood plains are closed off, the river's flow downstream becomes more violent and uncontrollable; by turning complex river systems into the simple mechanics of a water pipe, engineers have often created danger where they promised safety. The Rhine, Europe's most engineered river, is a good example. For a long time engineers have erased its backwaters and cut it off from its plain. The aim was partly to improve navigation, and partly to speed floodwaters out of the Alps and down to the North Sea. Now, when it rains in the Alps, the peak flows from several branches of the Rhine coincide where once they arrived separately, and with four-fifths of the Lower Rhine's flood plain barricaded off, the waters rise. The result is more frequent flooding and greater damage. The same thing has happened in the US on the Mississippi River, which drains the world's second-largest river catchment into the Gulf of Mexico. Despite some $7 billion spent over the last century on levees (embankments), the situation is growing worse.

Specialists in water control now say that a new approach is needed — one which takes the whole landscape into consideration. To help keep London's feet dry, the UK Environment Agency is reflooding 10 square kilometres of the ancient flood plain of the River Thames outside Oxford. Nearer to London, it has spent £100 million creating new wetlands and a relief channel across 16 kilometres of flood plain. Similar ideas are being tested in Austria, in one of Europe's largest river restorations to date. The engineers calculate that the restored flood plain of the Drava River can now store up to 10 million cubic metres of floodwater, and slow down storm surges coming out of the Alps by more than an hour, protecting towns not only in Austria, but as far downstream as Slovenia and Croatia.

The Dutch, for whom preventing floods is a matter of survival, have gone furthest. This nation, built largely on drained marshes and seabed, has had several severe shocks in the last two decades, when very large numbers of people have had to be evacuated. Since that time, the Dutch have broken one of their most enduring national stereotypes by allowing engineers to punch holes in dykes. They plan to return up to a sixth of the country to its former waterlogged state in order to better protect the rest.

Water use in cities also needs to change. At the moment, cities seem to create floods; they are concreted and paved so that rains flow quickly into rivers. A new breed of ‘soft engineers' wants cities to be porous; Berlin is one place where this is being done. Tough new rules for new developments mean that drains will be prevented from becoming overloaded after heavy rains. Architects of new urban buildings are diverting rainwater from the roofs for use in toilets and the irrigation of roof gardens, while water falling onto the ground is collected in ponds, or passes underground through porous paving. One high-tech urban development can store a sixth of its annual rainfall, and reuse most of the rest.

Could this be expanded to protect a whole city? The test case could be Los Angeles. With non-porous surfaces covering 70% of the city, drainage is a huge challenge. Billions of dollars have been spent digging huge drains and concreting riverbeds, but many communities still flood regularly. Meanwhile, this desert city ships water from hundreds of kilometres away to fill its taps and swimming pool. Los Angeles has recently launched a new scheme to utilise floodwater in the Sun Valley section of the city. The plan is to catch the rain that falls on thousands of driveways, parking lots and rooftops in the valley. Trees will soak up water from parking lots; houses and public buildings will capture roof water to irrigate gardens and parks, and road drains will empty into old gravel pits to recharge the city's underground water reserves. Result: less flooding and more water for the city. It may sound expensive, until we realise how much is spent trying to drain cities and protect areas from flooding, and how little this method achieves.
Which paragraph contains the following information?
, in boxes 14–19 on your answer sheet.
how legislation has forced building designers to improve water use
two reasons why one river was isolated from its flood plain
how natural watercourses in the past assisted flood control
an example of flood control on one river, affecting three countries
a country which has partly destroyed one of its most typical features in order to control water
the writer's comment on the comparative cost-effectiveness of traditional flood control and newer methods

explanationSection.text:
答案：F
解析：F段提到“Tough new rules for new developments...”（对新开发的严格新规），这即是“legislation”（立法），并具体说明了建筑师如何被要求“diverting rainwater...”（引流雨水），这正是改进用水的措施。
答案：C
解析：C段明确给出了莱茵河被改造的两个原因：“The aim was partly to improve navigation, and partly to speed floodwaters...”。
答案：B
解析：B段描述了过去河流“took a winding path to the sea, floodwaters lost force and volume while meandering across flood plains...”，解释了自然蜿蜒的水道如何通过减缓水流和分散水量来帮助控制洪水。
答案：D
解析：D段提到了德拉瓦河（Drava River）的修复项目，并明确指出其保护范围不仅包括奥地利（Austria），还远及下游的斯洛文尼亚（Slovenia）和克罗地亚（Croatia），共三个国家。
答案：E
解析：E段讲到以堤坝闻名的荷兰（Dutch），现在却允许工程师“punch holes in dykes”（在堤坝上打洞），这正是破坏其典型特征的行为。
答案：G
解析：G段末尾评论道：“It may sound expensive, until we realise how much is spent trying to drain cities... and how little this method achieves.”，通过对比新旧方法的投入与产出，明确评论了其成本效益。

items.text:
(1) 题目 14：how legislation has forced building designers to improve water use （法律如何迫使建筑设计师改进城市用水）
答案：F
解析：F段提到“Tough new rules for new developments...”（对新开发的严格新规），这即是“legislation”（立法），并具体说明了建筑师如何被要求“diverting rainwater...”（引流雨水），这正是改进用水的措施。

(2) 题目 15：two reasons why one river was isolated from its flood plain （说明某条河为何与其洪泛平原隔离的两个原因）
答案：C
解析：C段明确给出了莱茵河被改造的两个原因：“The aim was partly to improve navigation, and partly to speed floodwaters...”。

(3) 题目 16：how natural watercourses in the past assisted flood control （过去的自然水路如何帮助防洪）
答案：B
解析：B段描述了过去河流“took a winding path to the sea, floodwaters lost force and volume while meandering across flood plains...”，解释了自然蜿蜒的水道如何通过减缓水流和分散水量来帮助控制洪水。

(4) 题目 17：an example of flood control on one river, affecting three countries （在一条河上实施的防洪实例，其影响波及三个国家）
答案：D
解析：D段提到了德拉瓦河（Drava River）的修复项目，并明确指出其保护范围不仅包括奥地利（Austria），还远及下游的斯洛文尼亚（Slovenia）和克罗地亚（Croatia），共三个国家。

(5) 题目 18：a country which has partly destroyed one of its most typical features in order to control water （一个国家为治水而部分破坏其最典型国土特征之一）
答案：E
解析：E段讲到以堤坝闻名的荷兰（Dutch），现在却允许工程师“punch holes in dykes”（在堤坝上打洞），这正是破坏其典型特征的行为。

(6) 题目 19：the writer's comment on the comparative cost-effectiveness of traditional flood control and newer methods （作者对传统防洪与新方法成本效益的比较性评论）
答案：G
解析：G段末尾评论道：“It may sound expensive, until we realise how much is spent trying to drain cities... and how little this method achieves.”，通过对比新旧方法的投入与产出，明确评论了其成本效益。

children.text: of these statements are true of the new approach to flood control?
A It aims to slow the movement of water to the sea.
B It aims to channel water more directly into rivers.
C It will cost more than twice as much as former measures.
D It will involve the loss of some areas of land.
E It has been tested only in the Netherlands.

explanationSection.text:
答案：A（It is designed to slow down the flow of water into the sea. 它旨在减缓水流入海的速度）
解析：B段提到新方法旨在通过"dispersing it... reviving river bends and marshes to curb the flow"（分散水流、恢复河湾和沼泽来遏制水流），这些都是减慢水流速度的措施。
答案：D（It will involve the loss/surrender of some land. 它将导致部分土地的损失/出让）
解析：D段和E段都提到了"reflooding"（重新淹没）土地和"return... to its former waterlogged state"（恢复被水淹没的状态），这都意味着将部分土地的使用权还给水。

items.text:
（1）题目 20：According to the article, which TWO of these statements are true of the new approach to flood control? （根据文章，关于防洪新方法的哪两项陈述是正确的？）
答案：A（It is designed to slow down the flow of water into the sea. 它旨在减缓水流入海的速度）
解析：B段提到新方法旨在通过"dispersing it... reviving river bends and marshes to curb the flow"（分散水流、恢复河湾和沼泽来遏制水流），这些都是减慢水流速度的措施。

（2）题目 21：According to the article, which TWO of these statements are true of the new approach to flood control? （根据文章，关于防洪新方法的哪两项陈述是正确的？）
答案：D（It will involve the loss/surrender of some land. 它将导致部分土地的损失/出让）
解析：D段和E段都提到了"reflooding"（重新淹没）土地和"return... to its former waterlogged state"（恢复被水淹没的状态），这都意味着将部分土地的使用权还给水。

children.text: from the passage for each answer.
Some of the most severe floods for many centuries have recently occurred in parts of
river have experienced similar problems with water control.
An area near Oxford will be flooded to protect the city of
Planners who wish to allow water to pass more freely through city surfaces are called
A proposal for part of the city of
could show whether small-scale water projects could be applied on a large scale.

explanationSection.text:
答案：central Europe
解析：A段首句直接提到“winter floods on the rivers of central Europe have been among the worst for 600 to 700 years”。
答案：Mississippi
解析：C段在描述了莱茵河的问题后，紧接着说“The same thing has happened in the US on the Mississippi River...”。
答案：London
解析：D段提到“To help keep London's feet dry, the UK Environment Agency is reflooding... outside Oxford.”。
答案：soft engineers
解析：F段提到“A new breed of ‘soft engineers’ wants cities to be porous...”。
答案：Los Angeles
解析：G段明确指出“The test case could be Los Angeles... a new scheme... in the Sun Valley section of the city.”，即以洛杉矶的一个片区作为检验大规模应用可能性的试点。

items.text:
(1) 题目 22：Some of the most severe floods for many centuries have recently occurred in parts of \___\___. （近几个世纪最严重的洪水之一最近发生在哪一地区）
答案：central Europe
解析：A段首句直接提到“winter floods on the rivers of central Europe have been among the worst for 600 to 700 years”。

(2) 题目 23：The Rhine and the \___\___ river have experienced similar problems with water control. （莱茵河与哪条河经历了类似的治水问题）
答案：Mississippi
解析：C段在描述了莱茵河的问题后，紧接着说“The same thing has happened in the US on the Mississippi River...”。

(3) 题目 24：An area near Oxford will be flooded to protect the city of \___\___. （牛津附近的一个地区将被蓄/淹以保护哪座城市）
答案：London
解析：D段提到“To help keep London's feet dry, the UK Environment Agency is reflooding... outside Oxford.”。

(4) 题目 25：Planners who wish to allow water to pass more freely through city surfaces are called \___\___. （允许城市表面更自由渗水的规划者被称作什么）
答案：soft engineers
解析：F段提到“A new breed of ‘soft engineers’ wants cities to be porous...”。

(5) 题目 26：A proposal for part of the city of \___\___ could show whether small-scale water projects could be applied on a large scale. （哪座城市的一个片区提案可检验小尺度项目能否大规模应用）
答案：Los Angeles
解析：G段明确指出“The test case could be Los Angeles... a new scheme... in the Sun Valley section of the city.”，即以洛杉矶的一个片区作为检验大规模应用可能性的试点。
